does: This paper proposes a method called rewarded soup that aims to achieve Pareto-optimal alignment of foundation models across diverse reward objectives. The approach involves fine-tuning multiple copies of a pre-trained model independently, each on a different reward function (e.g., different human preferences or alignment objectives), and then linearly interpolating the resulting weight matrices to create models that balance multiple objectives. The key empirical finding is that weights fine-tuned on diverse rewards from a shared pre-trained initialization remain linearly connected in weight space, enabling simple weighted averaging to produce models that generalize well across different preference dimensions. The method is evaluated on both text tasks (summarization, question answering, helpful assistant responses) and text-image tasks, demonstrating improvements in balancing multiple objectives compared to single-reward training approaches.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research on RLHF and reward modeling for aligning large language models, responding to the fundamental challenge that real-world reward functions are imperfect proxies and human preferences are inherently diverse and sometimes contradictory. It extends recent findings about the linear mode connectivity of neural networks fine-tuned from shared initializations, applying these geometric insights to the alignment problem. The paper connects to multi-objective optimization literature and Pareto efficiency concepts, bringing these classical optimization ideas to modern deep learning. It also relates to research on mixture-of-experts and ensemble methods, though applied to weight interpolation rather than parallel model execution. The core insight about linear connectivity of fine-tuned weights builds on and validates prior observations about neural network loss landscapes and the structure of models trained from common starting points.
enables: This work opens new directions for handling the inherent multi-objective nature of AI alignment without requiring explicit trade-off decisions beforehand. By showing that Pareto-optimal solutions can be accessed through simple weight interpolation, the method enables deployment scenarios where different stakeholders or use cases can select points along the Pareto frontier without retraining. This could facilitate more flexible and user-centric alignment where models can be dynamically adjusted to different preference balances at inference time. The approach also suggests deeper investigation into the geometric structure of fine-tuned model weights and why linear interpolation preserves diverse capabilities. Future work could explore whether this framework extends to even larger numbers of diverse objectives, non-linear interpolation schemes, or continuous preference specifications. The method potentially enables more principled approaches to multi-stakeholder AI systems and could influence how we think about building plural, rather than singular, objective alignment into deployed models.
